summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an authenticated WebSocket client can inject arbitrary OS commands through the unsanitized projectPath parameter in the shell init message, achieving command execution on the server host.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the injected OS command (touch /tmp/evohunt_cmdi_proof) to execute on the server host via the bash command substitution in projectPath, creating the proof file.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the target service, registered a user, obtained a JWT via the hardcoded default secret, connected to the /shell WebSocket with a projectPath containing $(touch /tmp/evohunt_cmdi_proof), and verified that the proof file was created on the host filesystem, confirming command injection.
verification_method: The exploit script connects to the /shell WebSocket with a projectPath containing a bash command substitution that creates a unique proof file at /tmp/evohunt_cmdi_proof. After sending the WebSocket init message, the script checks for the existence of this proof file. The check distinguishes actual command injection from a generic WebSocket error or server startup issue.
evidence_produced_by_run: The WebSocket init message with injected projectPath was accepted by the shell handler.
The bash command substitution in projectPath executed on the host, creating /tmp/evohunt_cmdi_proof.
The proof file /tmp/evohunt_cmdi_proof was found after the exploit ran, confirming arbitrary command execution.
The proof string EVOHUNT_CMDI_CONFIRMED was emitted by the exploit script.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
